head: die Handynummer /ňąh…õndiňĆn äm…ź/
gloss: Mobile phone number; cell phone number.
notes: ‚ÄĘ das Handy = mobile phone / cell phone; die Nummer = number as an ID/code.
‚ÄĘ die Handynummer is specifically a mobile number; die Telefonnummer can be any phone number.

examples.english: Do you have Anne's mobile number?

head: die Zahlen ab 11 /diňź ňątsaňźl…ôn ap …õlf/
notes: ‚ÄĘ 13-19 mostly use -zehn: dreizehn, vierzehn, f√ľnfzehn; watch the shortened forms sechzehn and siebzehn.
‚ÄĘ 21-99 put the small number first, then und, then the ten: einundzwanzig = one-and-twenty = twenty-one.
‚ÄĘ In listening, zwo may be used for zwei, hundert for einhundert, and tausend for eintausend.
‚ÄĘ Million and Milliarde are nouns, so they use eine: eine Million, eine Milliarde.

head: mehr /meňź…źŐĮ/
notes: ‚ÄĘ mehr adds quantity: mehr Zahlen = more numbers.
‚ÄĘ Opposite idea: weniger = less / fewer.

examples.english: More numbers again? / Even more numbers?

head: noch einmal /n…Ēx ňąa…™ŐĮnmaňźl/
notes: ‚ÄĘ noch = still / yet / additionally; einmal = once / one time.
‚ÄĘ Together, noch einmal means one more time or again. Casual shorter form: nochmal.

examples.german: H√∂ren Sie das Gespr√§ch noch einmal.
examples.english: Listen to the conversation one more time.

examples.german: Bitte wiederholen Sie das noch einmal.
examples.english: Please repeat that one more time.
More numbers again? / Even more numbers?

head: der Notizzettel /noňątiňźtsňĆts…õtlŐ©/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Correct spelling: Notizzettel, not notizzetel/nottizetel.
‚ÄĘ die Notiz = note; der Zettel = slip/piece of paper. Together: der Notizzettel = a note sheet.

examples.german: Erg√§nzen Sie den Notizzettel. (den because masculine + accusative.)
H√∂ren Sie das Gespr√§ch noch einmal und erg√§nzen Sie den Notizzettel.
examples.english: Listen to the conversation one more time and complete the note sheet.

examples.german: Der Notizzettel liegt auf dem Tisch.
examples.english: The note slip is on the table.

head: Tsch√ľss /t É Źs/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Tsch√ľss is the common German casual goodbye, especially in everyday speech.

head: Tschau /t Éa ä/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Tschau is the German spelling of Italian ciao; it is casual, like English bye.
‚ÄĘ Tsch√ľss and Tschau feel similar because both are casual goodbyes and both start with tsch, but they are different words.

head: sp√§ter /ňą Ép…õňźt…ź/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Opposite idea: fr√ľh = early; sp√§ter = later. Related adjective: sp√§t = late.
‚ÄĘ In bis sp√§ter, German literally says until later; natural English is see you later.

examples.english: Early today, later in the evening.

head: bis /b…™s/
notes: ‚ÄĘ In farewell formulas, bis means until: bis sp√§ter = until later / see you later.

head: dann /dan/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Use dann to order actions in time: first one thing, then the next.
‚ÄĘ In a normal statement, dann can stand first; the conjugated verb then comes right after it.

examples.german: Zuerst lernen wir Zahlen, dann lesen wir den Text.
examples.english: First we learn numbers, then we read the text.

examples.german: Ich trinke Kaffee, dann gehe ich zum Kurs.
examples.english: I drink coffee, then I go to the course.

examples.german: Wenn du Zeit hast, dann komm bitte.
examples.english: If you have time, then please come.

head: die Vorwahl /ňąfoňź…źŐĮvaňźl/
notes: ‚ÄĘ One compound word: Vorwahl, not two words.
‚ÄĘ In 0621 / 53487, 0621 is the Vorwahl and 53487 is the local number.

examples.english: What area code does Berlin have?

head: k√∂nnen /ňąkŇďn…ôn/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Modal-verb pattern: the conjugated modal verb takes the normal verb position, and the main verb stays as an infinitive at the end.
‚ÄĘ In yes/no questions, the conjugated modal comes first: Kannst du ... wiederholen?
‚ÄĘ In Kannst du das bitte wiederholen?, das is the object (ŔÖŔĀōĻŔąŔĄ) of the sentence (ō¨ŔÖŔĄŔá): the thing being repeated. Bitte means please and can move around.

examples.english: Can you please speak slowly? [formal you]

head: wiederholen /viňźd…źňąhoňźl…ôn/
gloss: To repeat; say or do again.

head: wieder /ňąviňźd…ź/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Do not confuse wieder (again) with wider (against).

examples.german: Bis morgen! Wir sehen uns wieder.
examples.english: See you tomorrow! We'll see each other again.

head: die Telefonnummer /teňąlefoňźnňĆn äm…ź/
notes: ‚ÄĘ die Nummer + das Telefon becomes die Telefonnummer: a compound noun with the gender of Nummer.
‚ÄĘ In German, ask Wie ist deine Telefonnummer? literally: How is your phone number?

examples.german: Das ist die Telefonnummer von Maria.

head: die Stra√üe /ňą Ét Āaňźs…ô/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Standard German spelling uses √ü: Stra√üe. In Switzerland, it is usually written Strasse.
‚ÄĘ In der Bergstra√üe uses Dativ: wohnen answers where?; with location, in + feminine die Stra√üe becomes in der Stra√üe.

examples.german: Ich wohne in Mannheim in der Bergstra√üe 10.
examples.english: I live in Mannheim at 10 Bergstra√üe.

head: wohnen /ňąvoňźn…ôn/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Use wohnen for where someone lives: Ich wohne in Berlin.
‚ÄĘ Common question: Wo wohnen Sie? = Where do you live? [formal you]

head: gern /…°…õ Ān/
notes: ‚ÄĘ gern and gerne mean the same thing; gerne sounds a little fuller/softer.
‚ÄĘ With a verb, gern often translates naturally as like to: Ich lerne gern = I like learning.

examples.english: Georg is coming for the meal today.

head: zum /ts äm/
gloss: To the; for the; contraction of zu + dem.
notes: ‚ÄĘ zum = zu + dem, used before masculine or neuter dative nouns.
‚ÄĘ In zum Essen, Essen is used as a noun: for the meal / to eat.

examples.english: Georg is coming for the meal today.
I am going to the course.

head: essen /ňą…õsnŐ©/
gloss: To eat; food or meal when capitalized as Essen.
notes: ‚ÄĘ Lowercase essen is the verb: to eat.
‚ÄĘ Capitalized Essen is a noun: food or a meal. So write zum Essen, not zum essen.

examples.english: Georg is coming for the meal today.

head: die Zahlen 0-10 /diňź ňątsaňźl…ôn n äl b…™s tseňźn/
notes: ‚ÄĘ eins is the counting form for 1; before a noun it usually becomes ein/eine.
‚ÄĘ In phone numbers, digits are often spoken one by one: 0612 = null sechs eins zwei.

examples.german: Meine Telefonnummer ist null sechs eins zwei.
examples.english: My phone number is zero six one two.

head: die Nummer /ňąn äm…ź/
gloss: Number as a label, code, or identifier.
notes: ‚ÄĘ Nummer is a number used as an ID or label: Telefonnummer, Hausnummer, Zimmer Nummer 12.
‚ÄĘ Difference: Zahl = numerical value/counting/math; Nummer = assigned number/code/identifier.

head: die Zahl /tsaňźl/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Zahl is a number as a value: counting, math, or a number you hear/write.
‚ÄĘ Compare Nummer: an assigned label/code/ID, as in Telefonnummer, Hausnummer, Zimmer Nummer 12.

head: von /f…Ēn/
notes: ‚ÄĘ In die Adresse von Anne, von shows whose address it is: Anne's address.
‚ÄĘ von always takes dative, but with names like Anne there is no visible article change.

examples.german: Die Adresse von Anne ist hier.
Das ist die Telefonnummer von Maria.

head: stehen /ňą Éteňź…ôn/
gloss: To stand; be located or positioned.
notes: ‚ÄĘ In word-order rules, stehen means a word ‚Äústands‚ÄĚ or ‚Äúgoes‚ÄĚ in a certain sentence position.
‚ÄĘ Das Verb steht auf Position 1 means the verb comes first; Position 2 means it comes second.

examples.german: In Ja-/Nein-Fragen steht das Verb auf Position 1.
examples.english: In yes/no questions, the verb comes first.

examples.german: In Antworten und W-Fragen steht das Verb auf Position 2.
examples.english: In answers and W-questions, the verb comes second.
A new car is parked there.

head: die Regel /ňą Āeňź…°lŐ©/
notes: ‚ÄĘ die Regeln is plural accusative here: the rules being completed.

head: schreiben /ňą É Āa…™ŐĮb…ôn/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Formal command: Schreiben Sie ... = Write ...; verb first, Sie capitalized.

examples.german: Schreiben Sie das in die Tabelle.
examples.english: Write that in the table. [formal you]

head: der Satz /zats/
notes: ‚ÄĘ die S√§tze is plural accusative here: the sentences being written.
‚ÄĘ Correct spelling: S√§tze with √§, not Satze.

examples.german: Lesen Sie die S√§tze noch einmal.
examples.english: Read the sentences one more time. [formal you]

head: L√§nder, Sprachen, Nationalit√§ten /ňąl…õnd…ź ňą Ép Āaňźx…ôn natsiŐĮonaliňąt…õňźt…ôn/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Nationality nouns are capitalized in German: der Schweizer, die Schweizerin.
‚ÄĘ Textbook shorthand Spanier/in means der Spanier / die Spanierin.

examples.german: Sie kommt aus Frankreich und spricht Franz√∂sisch.
examples.english: She comes from France and speaks French.

examples.german: Er ist Schweizer, sie ist Schweizerin.
examples.english: He is Swiss; she is Swiss.

examples.german: Er ist Spanier, sie ist Spanierin.
examples.english: He is Spanish; she is Spanish.

head: Viel Spa√ü! /fiňźl  Épaňźs/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Correct spelling in standard German: Spa√ü with √ü, not spas.

head: der Spa√ü / Épaňźs/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Long a + sharp s: Spa√ü. In Switzerland, √ü is usually written ss: Spass.

examples.english: That is a lot of fun.

head: viel /fiňźl/
gloss: Much; a lot; many before plural nouns.
notes: ‚ÄĘ In Viel Spa√ü!, viel intensifies the wish: literally much fun, naturally have fun.

examples.english: I have a lot of time.

head: zusammen /tsuňązam…ôn/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Spelling: zwei m und -en at the end ‚ÄĒ not zusamen (common typo/OCR).
‚ÄĘ In Hoi zusammen!, zusammen means ‚Äúeveryone / all of you‚ÄĚ ‚ÄĒ like English ‚ÄúHi all!‚ÄĚ

head: Hoi /h…Ē…™/
gloss: Informal hi or hey; especially typical in Switzerland.
notes: ‚ÄĘ One-word greeting (interjection), like Hallo or Servus ‚ÄĒ no article, no plural, no verb endings.
‚ÄĘ Very common in Swiss German contexts; also heard in casual speech elsewhere; not the standard classroom default.

head: die Argentinierin /a Ā…°…õnňątsiňźn…ô Ā…™n/
gloss: An Argentine; feminine die Argentinierin, masculine der Argentinier.
notes: ‚ÄĘ Same pattern as die Schweizerin (from die Schweiz): demonyms from Argentinien, the country name in German.

examples.german: Er ist Schweizer, sie ist Schweizerin; er ist Argentinier, sie ist Argentinierin.
examples.english: He is Swiss, she is Swiss; he is Argentine, she is Argentine.

examples.german: Sie ist Argentinierin und lebt in Berlin.
examples.english: She is Argentine and lives in Berlin.

examples.german: Zwei Argentinierinnen und ein Argentinier arbeiten hier.
examples.english: Two Argentine women and one Argentine man work here.

head: Servus /ňąz…õ…źŐĮvus/
gloss: Informal hi or bye; strongest in Austria and southern Germany.
notes: ‚ÄĘ Informal greeting and goodbye; most typical in Austria, Bavaria, and other southern regions.
‚ÄĘ From Latin for ‚Äúyour servant‚ÄĚ; today it is a casual hi, hey, or bye in one short word.
‚ÄĘ Not the everyday norm everywhere in Germany; in the north people more often say Hallo or Tsch√ľss.

head: in /…™n/
gloss: In; inside; on a team (+ dative for location).
notes: ‚ÄĘ Wo? uses dative: in einem Team. Wohin? uses accusative: in ein Team.

examples.english: You all are on a team.
You all are joining / going into a team. [accusative: direction]

head: der neue Praktikant /deňź…źŐĮ ňąn…Ē Ź…ô p Āaktiňąkant/
gloss: The new intern; pattern for adjective endings after der.
notes: ‚ÄĘ neu becomes neue because it stands before a masculine nominative noun with der.
‚ÄĘ After der/die/das, use -e in nominative singular; use -en in most other definite-article forms.

examples.english: The intern is new. [after sein: no adjective ending]
I see the new intern. [accusative masculine: den neuen]

head: auch /a äŐĮx/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Often comes near what it adds: Ich auch = me too; auch ich = I too / even I.

head: Wie geht‚Äôs? /viňź …°eňźts/
gloss: How‚Äôs it going? / How are you?
notes: ‚ÄĘ Short for: Wie geht es? Full informal form: Wie geht es dir?
‚ÄĘ Common response: Und dir? (= Und wie geht es dir?)
‚ÄĘ Another common response: Mir geht‚Äôs gut. (= I‚Äôm doing well.)
‚ÄĘ Formally: Wie geht es Ihnen?

examples.english: How‚Äôs it going? / How are you?

head: Freut mich /f Ā…Ē ŹŐĮt m…™√ß/
gloss: Nice to meet you; pleased to meet you.
notes: ‚ÄĘ Short for Es freut mich: literally, it pleases me.
‚ÄĘ mich is a personal pronoun in the accusative here, not reflexive; the hidden subject is es.
‚ÄĘ Contrast: Ich freue mich means I am happy / pleased; there mich is reflexive because ich and mich refer to the same person.

examples.english: Nice to meet you. [personal-pronoun mich, accusative]

examples.german: Guten Tag, ich hei√üe Ali. Freut mich.
examples.english: Good day, my name is Ali. Nice to meet you.

head: Reflexivpronomen / Āefl…õňąksiňźfňĆp Āoňźnoňźm…ôn/
gloss: Reflexive pronouns: myself, yourself, himself, etc.
notes: ‚ÄĘ Reflexive means the subject and object are the same person: Ich wasche mich.
‚ÄĘ They follow person and case, not grammatical gender: er/sie/es all use sich.
‚ÄĘ Akkusativ forms: mich, dich, sich, sich, sich, uns, euch, sich, sich.
‚ÄĘ Dativ forms: mir, dir, sich, sich, sich, uns, euch, sich, sich.
‚ÄĘ Use accusative when the reflexive pronoun is the direct object; use dative when another accusative object is present: Ich wasche mich vs. Ich wasche mir die H√§nde.

examples.english: You all introduce yourselves. [euch; accusative reflexive]
I wash my hands. [mir; dative reflexive]
You buy yourself a book. [dir; dative reflexive]
She buys herself a coffee. [sich; dative reflexive]
It washes its hands. [sich; dative reflexive]

examples.german: Wir stellen uns eine Reise vor.
examples.english: We imagine a trip. [uns; dative reflexive]
You all buy yourselves tickets. [euch; dative reflexive]
They buy themselves tickets. [sich; dative reflexive]

examples.german: Stellen Sie sich eine Stadt vor.
examples.english: Imagine a city. [sich; formal dative reflexive]

head: Personalpronomen /p…õ ĀzoňąnaňźlňĆp Āoňźnoňźm…ôn/
gloss: Personal pronouns: ich, mich, mir; du, dich, dir; etc.
notes: ‚ÄĘ Pronouns replace nouns: der Mann ‚Üí er; die Frau ‚Üí sie; das Kind ‚Üí es.
‚ÄĘ Nominativ marks the subject: ich, du, er, sie, es, wir, ihr, sie.
‚ÄĘ Akkusativ marks the direct object: mich, dich, ihn, sie, es, uns, euch, sie.
‚ÄĘ Dativ marks the indirect object or object after dative verbs/prepositions: mir, dir, ihm, ihr, ihm, uns, euch, ihnen.
‚ÄĘ Reflexive pronouns can look the same as personal pronouns, but the subject and object are the same person: Ich liebe dich vs. Du liebst dich.

examples.english: I help it. [ihm; dative for a neuter noun]
They come from Germany. [sie; nominative plural]

head: sich vorstellen /z…™√ß ňąfoňź…źŐĮňĆ Ét…õl…ôn/
gloss: To introduce oneself; to imagine something.
notes: ‚ÄĘ Separable reflexive verb: vor moves to the end (Ich stelle mich vor.).
‚ÄĘ Introduce oneself uses accusative reflexive pronouns: mich, dich, sich, uns, euch, sich.
‚ÄĘ Imagine something uses dative reflexive pronouns with an object: mir, dir, sich, uns, euch, sich.

examples.german: Wir stellen uns im Kurs vor.
Stellen Sie sich eine Stadt vor.

head: begr√ľ√üen /b…ôňą…° ĀyňźsnŐ©/
gloss: To greet; say hello to someone.
notes: ‚ÄĘ Takes an accusative object: Begr√ľ√üen Sie Ihren Partner / Ihre Partnerin.

examples.english: Greet your partner. [male partner; formal instruction]
Greet your partner. [female partner; formal instruction]

head: der Partner / die Partnerin /ňąpa Ātn…ź/ /ňąpa Ātn…ô Ā…™n/

examples.english: Your partner is here. [male partner; nominative]

head: Wie hei√üt ...? /viňź ha…™ŐĮst/
notes: ‚ÄĘ W-question pattern: Wie is position 1, the finite verb hei√üt is position 2.

examples.german: Wie hei√üt Ihr Land in Ihrer Sprache?
examples.english: What is your country called in your language? [formal]
What is that called in German?

head: die Sprache /ňą Ép Āaňźx…ô/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Feminine noun: die Sprache; after static in, use dative: in der Sprache / in Ihrer Sprache.

examples.german: In Ihrer Sprache hei√üt das anders.
examples.english: In your language, that is called something different. [formal]
I am learning the German language.

head: Ihr- /iňź…źŐĮ/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Capitalized Ihr- means formal your. Endings follow ein-words: Ihr Land, Ihre Sprache, in Ihrer Sprache.

examples.english: I am learning your language. [formal; accusative]
I speak in your language. [formal; dative]

head: fliegen /ňąfliňź…°…ôn/
gloss: To fly; travel by plane (+ nach ‚Ä¶ / zu ‚Ä¶).

head: reisen /ňą Āa…™ŐĮznŐ©/
gloss: To travel; go on a trip (+ nach ‚Ä¶ without article; + in die ‚Ä¶ when an article appears).

head: fahren /ňąfaňź Ā…ôn/
gloss: To drive; ride; go by transport (+ nach ‚Ä¶ for many destinations).
notes: ‚ÄĘ Strong verb: er f√§hrt; Pr√§teritum fuhr ‚ÄĒ expect this in texts and exams.

examples.english: We're going to Munich by car / bus / train.
Where are you going? [formal, destination ask]

head: gehen /ňą…°eňź…ôn/
gloss: To go; walk (+ zu / nach / + place; also set phrases).
notes: ‚ÄĘ Irregular present: ich gehe, du gehst, er geht; for whole countries people often prefer fahren/reisen/fliegen.

head: aber /ňąaňźb…ź/
gloss: but; however ‚ÄĒ introduces a contrast or exception
notes: ‚ÄĘ Coordinating conjunction: word order in the following clause is unchanged (verb stays in position 2).

examples.german: Ich komme aus Deutschland, aber er kommt aus der Schweiz.
examples.english: I come from Germany, but he comes from Switzerland.

examples.german: Die meisten L√§nder haben keinen Artikel ‚ÄĒ aber die Schweiz, die T√ľrkei und die Niederlande schon.
examples.english: Most countries have no article ‚ÄĒ but Switzerland, Turkey, and the Netherlands do.

head: auffallen /ňąa äŐĮfňĆfal…ôn/
gloss: To stand out; be noticeable / catch the eye.
notes: ‚ÄĘ Separable: auf sits at the end of the clause (Was f√§llt auf?).

examples.english: What do you notice? / What stands out? [reflection question]
The photo stands out; it catches your eye.

head: markieren /ma Āňąkiňź Ā…ôn/
gloss: To mark; highlight verbs or phrases in the text (+ acc.).

examples.german: Markieren Sie den Infinitiv im Text.
examples.english: Mark the infinitive in the passage [instruction].

head: hei√üen /ňąha…™ŐĮsnŐ©/
gloss: To be called; one‚Äôs name is (3rd sg. er/sie/es hei√üt).
notes: ‚ÄĘ OCR often miswrites hei√üen as heisen; spelling is hei√üen (√ü after long diphthong).

head: die W-Frage /ňąveňźňĆfraňź…°…ô/
gloss: WH-question: starts with Wer, Was, Wo, wie, Wann ‚Ä¶
notes: ‚ÄĘ The question word fills slot 1; the finite verb is still second (verb-second), then the rest.

head: die Antwort /ňąantňĆv…Ē Āt/
gloss: Answer; reply (to a question or task).

examples.german: Schreiben Sie die Antwort in den Satz.
examples.english: Write the answer into the sentence.

examples.german: Sammeln Sie die Verbformen in die Tabelle.
examples.english: Collect the verb forms into the table [class instruction].

examples.german: Sammeln Sie die Verbformen in die Tabelle.
examples.english: Collect the verb forms into the table.

examples.german: Schreiben Sie das in die Tabelle.

head: die Verbform /ňąf…Ē Ām/
gloss: Verb form (inflected shape of a lemma).
notes: ‚ÄĘ Misread Verbendung: the word is Verbformen.

examples.german: Sammeln Sie die Verbformen in die Tabelle.
examples.english: Collect the verb forms into the table.

head: auf einen Blick /a äŐĮf ňąa…™ŐĮn…ôn ňąbl…™k/

examples.german: Das ist die √úbersicht auf einen Blick.
examples.english: That's the overview at a glance.

head: das Verb /v…õ Āp/
gloss: Verb (word class for actions and states).

examples.german: Und ich bin Frau Lange, die Sekret√§rin.
examples.english: And I am Ms. Lange, the secretary.

head: die Frau /fra äŐĮ/
gloss: Ms. or Mrs. before a surname.

examples.german: Und ich bin Frau Lange, die Sekret√§rin.
examples.english: And I am Ms. Lange, the secretary.

head: aus /a äŐĮs/
gloss: From; out of (+ dative for origin).

head: Gr√ľ√ü Gott! /ňą…° Ā Źs ňą…°…Ēt/
gloss: Polite daytime hello‚ÄĒtypical in Austria and southern Germany.
notes: ‚ÄĘ Elsewhere Guten Tag is often the default; Hallo is informal.

examples.german: Lesen Sie das Gespr√§ch und erg√§nzen Sie.
examples.english: Read the dialogue [aloud/on the page], then finish / fill it in.

examples.german: Lesen Sie die √úberschrift zu Beginn der √úbung.
examples.english: Read the headline at the start of the exercise.
Two children listen to the dialogue.

examples.german: Das Bild passt zu diesem Text.
examples.english: The picture goes with / fits this text passage.
The second photo does not fit / is wrong.
The photo is sharp / in focus.

head: ankreuzen /ňąank Ā…Ē Źts…ôn/
notes: ‚ÄĘ The prefix an- moves to the end of the clause: Kreuzen Sie die richtige L√∂sung an.
‚ÄĘ Participle angekreuzt (perfect often: habe ‚Ä¶ angekreuzt).

examples.german: Kreuzen Sie die richtige L√∂sung an.
examples.english: Please tick the correct answer / solution.
I listen to the radio in the mornings.

examples.german: H√∂ren Sie bitte noch einmal das Gespr√§ch.
examples.english: Please listen to the dialogue one more time.

head: neu /n…Ē Ź/
gloss: New (opposite alt); before a noun, add an ending.

examples.english: A new car is parked there.

head: sein /za…™n/
notes: ‚ÄĘ Complement after sein is usually nominative. Present: ich bin ¬∑ du bist ¬∑ ist ¬∑ wir/sie/Sie sind.

examples.german: Und ich bin Frau Lange, die Sekret√§rin.
examples.english: And I am Ms. Lange, the secretary.
You all are on a team.

head: der Praktikant /p Āaktiňąkant/
gloss: Intern; Praktikantin is the feminine form.

examples.german: Ich mache ein Praktikum in Deutschland.
examples.english: I am doing an internship in Germany.

examples.german: Er ist Praktikant bei dieser Firma.
examples.english: He is an intern at this company.
